From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Formal investiture and crowning ceremony

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Coronation_of_George_VI_1937.jpg/330px-Coronation_of_George_VI_1937.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coronation_of_George_VI_1937.jpg)

[George VI](/wiki/George_VI "George VI") receiving the homage after being crowned in 1937; [watercolour](/wiki/Watercolour "Watercolour") by [Henry Charles Brewer](/wiki/Henry_Charles_Brewer "Henry Charles Brewer")

The [coronation](/wiki/Coronation "Coronation") of the [monarch of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Monarch_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarch of the United Kingdom") is an [initiation](/wiki/Initiation "Initiation") ceremony in which they are formally invested with [regalia](/wiki/Regalia "Regalia") and crowned at [Westminster Abbey](/wiki/Westminster_Abbey "Westminster Abbey"). It corresponds to the coronations that formerly took place in other [European monarchies](/wiki/European_monarchies "European monarchies"), which have all abandoned coronations in favour of [inauguration](/wiki/Inauguration "Inauguration") or [enthronement](/wiki/Enthronement "Enthronement") ceremonies. A coronation is a symbolic formality and does not signify the official beginning of the monarch's reign; *[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* and *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* his or her reign commences from the moment of the preceding monarch's death or abdication, maintaining legal continuity of the monarchy.

The coronation usually takes place several months after the death of the monarch's predecessor, as it is considered a joyous occasion that would be inappropriate while mourning continues. This interval also gives planners enough time to complete the required elaborate arrangements. The [most recent coronation](/wiki/Coronation_of_Charles_III_and_Camilla "Coronation of Charles III and Camilla") took place on 6 May 2023 to crown [King Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III") and [Queen Camilla](/wiki/Queen_Camilla "Queen Camilla").

The ceremony is performed by the [archbishop of Canterbury](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Canterbury "Archbishop of Canterbury"), the most senior cleric in the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"), of which the monarch is [supreme governor](/wiki/Supreme_Governor_of_the_Church_of_England "Supreme Governor of the Church of England"). Other clergy and members of the [British nobility](/wiki/British_nobility "British nobility") traditionally have roles as well. Most participants wear ceremonial uniforms or robes, and before the most recent coronation, some wore [coronets](/wiki/Coronet "Coronet"). Many government officials and guests attend, including representatives of other countries.

The essential elements of the coronation have remained largely unchanged for the past 1,000 years. The sovereign is first presented to, and acclaimed by, the people. The sovereign then swears an oath to uphold the law and the Church. Following that, the monarch is [anointed](/wiki/Anointing "Anointing") with [holy oil](/wiki/Holy_anointing_oil "Holy anointing oil"), invested with regalia, and crowned, before receiving the [homage](/wiki/Homage_(feudal) "Homage (feudal)") of their subjects. [Consorts](/wiki/Queen_consort "Queen consort") of kings are then anointed and crowned as queens. The service ends with a closing procession, and since the 20th century it has been traditional for the [royal family](/wiki/British_Royal_Family "British Royal Family") to appear later on the balcony of [Buckingham Palace](/wiki/Buckingham_Palace "Buckingham Palace") to greet crowds and watch a [flypast](/wiki/Flypast "Flypast").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [List of British coronations](/wiki/List_of_British_coronations "List of British coronations")

### English coronations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: English coronations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Bayeux_Tapestry_scene29-30-31_Harold_coronation.jpg/500px-Bayeux_Tapestry_scene29-30-31_Harold_coronation.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bayeux_Tapestry_scene29-30-31_Harold_coronation.jpg)

Coronation of [Harold II](/wiki/Harold_Godwinson "Harold Godwinson") at [Westminster Abbey](/wiki/Westminster_Abbey "Westminster Abbey") in 1066, from the [Bayeux Tapestry](/wiki/Bayeux_Tapestry "Bayeux Tapestry")

English coronations were traditionally held at [Westminster Abbey](/wiki/Westminster_Abbey "Westminster Abbey"), with the monarch seated on the [Coronation Chair](/wiki/Coronation_Chair "Coronation Chair"). Main elements of the coronation service and the earliest form of oath can be traced to the ceremony devised by [Saint Dunstan](/wiki/Dunstan "Dunstan") for [King Edgar](/wiki/Edgar_of_England "Edgar of England")'s coronation in 973 AD at [Bath Abbey](/wiki/Bath_Abbey "Bath Abbey"). It drew on ceremonies used by the kings of the [Franks](/wiki/Franks "Franks") and those used in the [ordination](/wiki/Ordination "Ordination") of [bishops](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop"). Two versions of coronation services, known as *ordines* (from the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") *ordo*, meaning 'order') or [recensions](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/recension "wikt:recension"), survive from before the [Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest"). It is not known if the first recension was ever used in England, and it was the second recension which was used by Edgar in 973 and by subsequent [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon "Anglo-Saxon") and early [Norman](/wiki/Normans "Normans") kings.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Coronation_Henry4_England_01.jpg/500px-Coronation_Henry4_England_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coronation_Henry4_England_01.jpg)

Coronation of [Henry IV](/wiki/Henry_IV_of_England "Henry IV of England") at Westminster Abbey in 1399

A third recension was probably compiled during the reign of [Henry I](/wiki/Henry_I_of_England "Henry I of England") and was used at the coronation of his successor, [Stephen](/wiki/Stephen_of_England "Stephen of England"), in 1135. While retaining the most important elements of the Anglo-Saxon rite, it may have borrowed from the consecration of the [Holy Roman emperor](/wiki/Holy_Roman_emperor "Holy Roman emperor") from the *[Pontificale Romano-Germanicum](/wiki/Pontificale_Romano-Germanicum "Pontificale Romano-Germanicum")*, a book of German liturgy compiled in [Mainz](/wiki/Mainz "Mainz") in 961, thus bringing the English tradition into line with continental practice.[[2]](#cite_note-2) It remained in use until the coronation of [Edward II](/wiki/Edward_II "Edward II") in 1308 when the fourth recension was first used, having been compiled over several preceding decades. Although influenced by its French counterpart, the new *ordo* focused on the balance between the monarch and his nobles and on the oath, neither of which concerned the absolutist French kings.[[3]](#cite_note-3) One manuscript of this recension is the *[Liber Regalis](/wiki/Liber_Regalis "Liber Regalis")* at Westminster Abbey which has come to be regarded as the definitive version.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Following the start of the [Reformation in England](/wiki/Reformation_in_England "Reformation in England"), the boy king [Edward VI](/wiki/Edward_VI "Edward VI") had been crowned in the first [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") coronation in 1547, during which Archbishop [Thomas Cranmer](/wiki/Thomas_Cranmer "Thomas Cranmer") preached a sermon against [idolatry](/wiki/Idolatry "Idolatry") and "the tyranny of the bishops of Rome". However, six years later, he was succeeded by his half-sister [Mary I](/wiki/Mary_I "Mary I"), who restored the [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") rite.[[5]](#cite_note-5) In 1559, [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I") underwent the [last English coronation under the auspices of the Catholic Church](/wiki/Coronation_of_Elizabeth_I "Coronation of Elizabeth I"); however, Elizabeth's insistence on changes to reflect her Protestant beliefs resulted in several bishops refusing to officiate at the service, and it was conducted by the low-ranking [bishop of Carlisle](/wiki/Bishop_of_Carlisle "Bishop of Carlisle"), [Owen Oglethorpe](/wiki/Owen_Oglethorpe "Owen Oglethorpe").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### Scottish coronations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Scottish coronations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Alexander_III_and_Ollamh_R%C3%ADgh.JPG/330px-Alexander_III_and_Ollamh_R%C3%ADgh.JPG)](/wiki/File:Alexander_III_and_Ollamh_R%C3%ADgh.JPG)

[Alexander III of Scotland](/wiki/Alexander_III_of_Scotland "Alexander III of Scotland") at his coronation aged eight at [Scone Abbey](/wiki/Scone_Abbey "Scone Abbey") in 1249, being greeted by the royal poet who will recite the king's genealogy

Scottish coronations were traditionally held at [Scone Abbey](/wiki/Scone_Abbey "Scone Abbey") in [Perthshire](/wiki/Perthshire "Perthshire"), with the monarch seated on the [Stone of Destiny](/wiki/Stone_of_Scone "Stone of Scone"). The original rituals were a fusion of ceremonies used by the kings of [Dál Riata](/wiki/D%C3%A1l_Riata "Dál Riata"), based on the inauguration of [Aidan](/wiki/%C3%81ed%C3%A1n_mac_Gabr%C3%A1in "Áedán mac Gabráin") by [Columba](/wiki/Columba "Columba") in 574, and by the [Picts](/wiki/Picts "Picts") from whom the Stone of Destiny came. A crown does not seem to have been used until the inauguration of [Alexander II](/wiki/Alexander_II_of_Scotland "Alexander II of Scotland") in 1214. The ceremony included the [laying on of hands](/wiki/Laying_on_of_hands "Laying on of hands") by a senior cleric and the recitation of the king's [genealogy](/wiki/Genealogy "Genealogy").[[7]](#cite_note-Thomas_pp.46-47-7) The [bishop of St Andrews](/wiki/Bishop_of_St_Andrews "Bishop of St Andrews") (from 1472 an archbishop) usually presided, but other bishops and archbishops also performed at some coronations.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

After the coronation of [John Balliol](/wiki/John_Balliol "John Balliol"), the Stone was taken to Westminster Abbey in 1296 and in 1300–1301 Edward I of England had it incorporated into the English [Coronation Chair](/wiki/Coronation_Chair "Coronation Chair").[[10]](#cite_note-abbey-10) Its first certain use at an English coronation was that of Henry IV in 1399.[[11]](#cite_note-11) [Pope John XXII](/wiki/Pope_John_XXII "Pope John XXII") in a [bull](/wiki/Papal_bull "Papal bull") of 1329 granted the kings of Scotland the right to be anointed and crowned.[[7]](#cite_note-Thomas_pp.46-47-7) No record exists of the exact form of the medieval rituals, but a later account exists of the coronation of the 17-month-old infant [James V](/wiki/James_V "James V") at [Stirling Castle](/wiki/Stirling_Castle "Stirling Castle") in 1513. The ceremony was held in a church, since demolished, within the castle walls and was conducted by the [archbishop of Glasgow](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Glasgow "Archbishop of Glasgow"), because the [archbishop of St Andrews](/wiki/Archbishop_of_St_Andrews "Archbishop of St Andrews") had been killed at the [Battle of Flodden](/wiki/Battle_of_Flodden "Battle of Flodden").[[12]](#cite_note-12) It is likely that the child would have been [knighted](/wiki/Knight "Knight") before the start of the ceremony.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The coronation itself started with a [sermon](/wiki/Sermon "Sermon"), followed by the anointing and crowning, then the coronation oath, in this case taken for the child by an unknown noble or priest, and finally an oath of fealty and acclamation by the congregation.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

[James VI](/wiki/James_VI "James VI") had been [crowned](/wiki/Coronation_of_James_VI_of_Scotland "Coronation of James VI of Scotland") in the [Church of the Holy Rude](/wiki/Church_of_the_Holy_Rude "Church of the Holy Rude") at [Stirling](/wiki/Stirling "Stirling") in 1567. After the [Union of the Crowns](/wiki/Union_of_the_Crowns "Union of the Crowns"), he was crowned at [Westminster Abbey on 25 July 1603](/wiki/Coronation_of_James_I_and_Anne "Coronation of James I and Anne"). His son [Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England") travelled north for a Scottish coronation at [Holyrood Abbey](/wiki/Holyrood_Abbey "Holyrood Abbey") in [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh") in 1633,[[15]](#cite_note-15) but caused consternation amongst the [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian") Scots by his insistence on elaborate [High Anglican](/wiki/High_Anglican "High Anglican") ritual, arousing "gryt feir of inbriginge of poperie".[[16]](#cite_note-16) [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") underwent a [simple Presbyterian coronation ceremony](/wiki/Scottish_coronation_of_Charles_II "Scottish coronation of Charles II") at [Scone](/wiki/Scone_Palace "Scone Palace") in 1651, but his brother [James VII and II](/wiki/James_VII_and_II "James VII and II") was never crowned in Scotland, although Scottish peers attended [his 1685 coronation in London](/wiki/Coronation_of_James_II_and_VII_and_Mary "Coronation of James II and VII and Mary"), setting a precedent for future ceremonies.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The coronation of Charles II was the last to take place in Scotland, and no bishop presided as the [episcopacy](/wiki/Episcopacy "Episcopacy") had been abolished; the *de facto* head of government, [Archibald Campbell, 1st Marquess of Argyll](/wiki/Archibald_Campbell,_1st_Marquess_of_Argyll "Archibald Campbell, 1st Marquess of Argyll"), crowned Charles instead.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### Modern coronations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Modern coronations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Coronation_Procession_of_James_II.jpg/250px-Coronation_Procession_of_James_II.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coronation_Procession_of_James_II.jpg)

Partial illustration of the State Procession prior to the Coronation of [James II](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England") and [Mary of Modena](/wiki/Mary_of_Modena "Mary of Modena") at Westminster, 23 April 1685

The *Liber Regalis* was translated into English for the first time for the coronation of James I in 1603, partly as a result of the [reformation in England](/wiki/Reformation_in_England "Reformation in England") requiring services to be understood by the people,[[19]](#cite_note-19) but also an attempt by [antiquarians](/wiki/Antiquarian "Antiquarian") to recover a lost English identity from before the Norman Conquest.[[20]](#cite_note-20) In 1685, James II, who was a Catholic, ordered a truncated version of the service omitting the [Eucharist](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist"), but this was restored for later monarchs. Only four years later, the service was again revised by [Henry Compton](/wiki/Henry_Compton_(bishop) "Henry Compton (bishop)") for the coronation of [William III](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England") and [Mary II](/wiki/Mary_II_of_England "Mary II of England").[[21]](#cite_note-21) The Latin text was resurrected for the 1714 coronation of the German-speaking [George I](/wiki/George_I_of_Great_Britain "George I of Great Britain"), since it was the only common language between the king and the clergy. Perhaps because the 1761 coronation of [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III") had been beset by "numerous mistakes and stupidities",[[22]](#cite_note-22) the next time around, spectacle overshadowed the religious aspect of the service. The [coronation of George IV](/wiki/Coronation_of_George_IV "Coronation of George IV") in 1821 was an expensive and lavish affair with a vast amount of money being spent on it.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

George's brother and successor [William IV](/wiki/William_IV "William IV") had to be persuaded to be crowned at all; [his coronation](/wiki/Coronation_of_William_IV_and_Adelaide "Coronation of William IV and Adelaide") at a time of economic depression in 1831 cost only one sixth of that spent on the previous event. Traditionalists threatened to boycott what they called a "[Half Crown](/wiki/Half_crown_(British_coin) "Half crown (British coin)")-nation".[[24]](#cite_note-Strong,_p._401-24) The king merely wore his robes over his uniform as [Admiral of the Fleet](/wiki/Admiral_of_the_Fleet_(Royal_Navy) "Admiral of the Fleet (Royal Navy)").[[25]](#cite_note-25) For this coronation, a number of economising measures were made which would set a precedent followed by future monarchs. The assembly of peers and ceremonial at Westminster Hall involving the presentation of the regalia to the monarch was eliminated. The procession from Westminster Hall to the Abbey on foot was likewise eliminated and in its place, a state procession by coach from [St James's Palace](/wiki/St_James%27s_Palace "St James's Palace") to the abbey was instituted, an important feature of the modern event.[[24]](#cite_note-Strong,_p._401-24) The coronation banquet after the service proper was also terminated.[[26]](#cite_note-strong374-26)

When [Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") was [crowned in 1838](/wiki/Coronation_of_Queen_Victoria "Coronation of Queen Victoria"), the service followed the pared-down precedent set by her uncle, and the under-rehearsed ceremonial, again presided over by [William Howley](/wiki/William_Howley "William Howley"), was marred by mistakes and accidents.[[27]](#cite_note-27) The music in the abbey was widely criticised in the press, only one new piece having been written for it, and the large choir and orchestra were badly coordinated.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

In the 20th century, liturgical scholars sought to restore the spiritual meaning of the ceremony by rearranging elements with reference to the medieval texts,[[29]](#cite_note-29) creating a "complex marriage of innovation and tradition".[[30]](#cite_note-30) The greatly increased pageantry of the state processions was intended to emphasise the strength and diversity of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire").[[31]](#cite_note-31)

### Bringing coronations to the people

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Bringing coronations to the people")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Coronation_Procession_of_Elizabeth_I_of_England_1559.jpg/500px-Coronation_Procession_of_Elizabeth_I_of_England_1559.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coronation_Procession_of_Elizabeth_I_of_England_1559.jpg)

Queen [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I") carried from her Coronation in a horse-borne litter, 15 January 1559

The idea of the need to gain popular support for a new monarch by making the ceremony a spectacle for ordinary people, started with the coronation in 1377 of [Richard II](/wiki/Richard_II_of_England "Richard II of England") who was a 10-year-old boy, thought unlikely to command respect simply by his physical appearance. On the day before the coronation, the boy king and his retinue were met outside the [City of London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London") by the [lord mayor](/wiki/Lord_Mayor_of_London "Lord Mayor of London"), [aldermen](/wiki/Alderman "Alderman") and the [livery companies](/wiki/Livery_companies "Livery companies"), and he was conducted to the [Tower of London](/wiki/Tower_of_London "Tower of London") where he spent the night in [vigil](/wiki/Vigil "Vigil"). The following morning, the king travelled on horseback in a great procession through the decorated city streets to Westminster. Bands played along the route, the [public conduits](/wiki/Fountain "Fountain") flowed with red and white wine, and an imitation castle had been built in [Cheapside](/wiki/Cheapside "Cheapside"), probably to represent the [New Jerusalem](/wiki/New_Jerusalem "New Jerusalem"), where a girl blew [gold leaf](/wiki/Gold_leaf "Gold leaf") over the king and offered him wine. Similar, or even more elaborate pageants continued until the coronation of [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_Great_Britain "Charles II of Great Britain") in 1661.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Charles's pageant was watched by [Samuel Pepys](/wiki/Samuel_Pepys "Samuel Pepys") who wrote: "So glorious was the show with gold and silver that we were not able to look at it". James II abandoned the tradition of the pageant to pay for jewels for his queen[[33]](#cite_note-33) and thereafter there was only a short procession on foot from [Westminster Hall](/wiki/Westminster_Hall "Westminster Hall") to the abbey. For the [coronation of William IV and Adelaide](/wiki/Coronation_of_William_IV_and_Adelaide "Coronation of William IV and Adelaide") in 1831, a state procession from [St James's Palace](/wiki/St_James%27s_Palace "St James's Palace") to the abbey was instituted, and this pageantry is an important feature of the modern event.[[24]](#cite_note-Strong,_p._401-24)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Coronation_of_George_V_1911_2.jpg/330px-Coronation_of_George_V_1911_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coronation_of_George_V_1911_2.jpg)

King [George V](/wiki/George_V "George V") and [Queen Mary](/wiki/Mary_of_Teck "Mary of Teck") seated on the Chairs of Estate in front of the royal box at [their coronation](/wiki/Coronation_of_George_V_and_Mary "Coronation of George V and Mary") in 1911. It was the first time any part of the service had been photographed.

In early modern coronations, the events inside the abbey were usually recorded by artists and published in elaborate [folio](/wiki/Folio "Folio") books of engravings,[[34]](#cite_note-Strong415-34) the last of these was published in 1905 depicting the coronation which had taken place three years earlier.[[35]](#cite_note-Strong432-35) Re-enactments of the ceremony were staged at London and provincial theatres; in 1761, a production featuring the Westminster Abbey choir at the [Royal Opera House](/wiki/Royal_Opera_House "Royal Opera House") in [Covent Garden](/wiki/Covent_Garden "Covent Garden") ran for three months after the real event.[[34]](#cite_note-Strong415-34) In 1902, a request to record the ceremony on a [gramophone record](/wiki/Gramophone_record "Gramophone record") was rejected, but Sir [Benjamin Stone](/wiki/John_Benjamin_Stone "John Benjamin Stone") photographed the procession into the abbey.[[35]](#cite_note-Strong432-35) Nine years later, at the [coronation of George V](/wiki/Coronation_of_George_V "Coronation of George V"), Stone was allowed to photograph the recognition, the presentation of the swords, and the homage.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

The [coronation of George VI](/wiki/Coronation_of_George_VI "Coronation of George VI") in 1937 was broadcast on radio by the [British Broadcasting Corporation](/wiki/British_Broadcasting_Corporation "British Broadcasting Corporation") (BBC), and parts of the service were filmed and shown in cinemas.[[37]](#cite_note-37) The state procession was shown [live](/wiki/Live_television "Live television") on the new [BBC Television Service](/wiki/BBC_Television_Service "BBC Television Service"), the first major [outside broadcast](/wiki/Outside_broadcast "Outside broadcast").[[38]](#cite_note-38) At [Elizabeth II's coronation](/wiki/Coronation_of_Queen_Elizabeth_II "Coronation of Queen Elizabeth II") in 1953, most of the proceedings inside the abbey were also televised by the BBC. Originally, events as far as the choir screen were to be televised live, with the remainder to be filmed and released later after any mishaps were edited out. This would prevent television viewers from seeing most of the highlights of the coronation, including the actual crowning, live; it led to controversy in the press and even questions in parliament.[[39]](#cite_note-39) The organising committee subsequently decided that the entire ceremony would be televised, except for the anointing and communion, which had also been excluded from photography at the last coronation. It was revealed 30 years later that the about-face was due to the personal intervention of the Queen. It is estimated that over 20 million people watched the broadcast in the United Kingdom. The coronation contributed to the increase of public interest in television, which rose significantly.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

### Commonwealth realms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Commonwealth realms")]

The need to include the various elements of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") in coronations was not considered until 1902, when it was attended by the prime ministers and [governors-general](/wiki/Governor_general "Governor general") of the [British Dominions](/wiki/British_Dominion "British Dominion"), by then almost completely [autonomous](/wiki/Autonomous "Autonomous"), and also by many of the rulers of the [Indian Princely States](/wiki/Indian_Princely_States "Indian Princely States") and the various [British Protectorates](/wiki/British_Protectorate "British Protectorate"). An [Imperial Conference](/wiki/Imperial_Conference "Imperial Conference") was held afterwards.[[41]](#cite_note-41) In 1911, the procession inside Westminster Abbey included the banners of the dominions, India and the [Home Nations](/wiki/Home_Nations "Home Nations"). By 1937, the [Statute of Westminster 1931](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_1931 "Statute of Westminster 1931") had made the dominions fully independent, and the wording of the coronation oath was amended to include their names and confine the elements concerning religion to the United Kingdom.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Crowning_of_Charles_III_%28modified%29.jpg/250px-Crowning_of_Charles_III_%28modified%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Crowning_of_Charles_III_(modified).jpg)

The first British coronation in the 21st century during the [coronation of Charles III and Camilla](/wiki/Coronation_of_Charles_III_and_Camilla "Coronation of Charles III and Camilla") (May 6, 2023)

Thus since 1937, the monarch has been simultaneously crowned as sovereign of several independent nations besides the United Kingdom, known since 1953 as the [Commonwealth realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realms "Commonwealth realms"). Elizabeth II was asked, for example: "Will you solemnly promise and swear to govern the Peoples of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, the Union of South Africa, Pakistan and Ceylon, and of your Possessions and other Territories to any of them belonging or pertaining, according to their respective laws and customs?"[[43]](#cite_note-oremus-43) In 2023, the oath was amended to avoid reciting the realms other than the United Kingdom during the [coronation of Charles III](/wiki/Coronation_of_Charles_III "Coronation of Charles III").[[44]](#cite_note-44)

## Preparations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Preparations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Westminster-Abbey.JPG/250px-Westminster-Abbey.JPG)](/wiki/File:Westminster-Abbey.JPG)

[Westminster Abbey](/wiki/Westminster_Abbey "Westminster Abbey") has been the traditional location for coronations since 1066.

### Timing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Timing")]

The timing of the coronation has varied throughout British history. [King Edgar's](/wiki/Edgar_the_Peaceful "Edgar the Peaceful") coronation was some 15 years after his accession in 959 and may have been intended to mark the high point of his reign, or that he reached the age of 30, the age at which [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") Christ was baptised.[[45]](#cite_note-45) Harold II was crowned on the day after the death of his predecessor, [Edward the Confessor](/wiki/Edward_the_Confessor "Edward the Confessor"), the rush probably reflecting the contentious nature of Harold's succession;[[46]](#cite_note-Strong_p38-46) whereas the first [Norman](/wiki/Norman_dynasty "Norman dynasty") monarch, [William I](/wiki/William_the_Conqueror "William the Conqueror"), was also [crowned on the day he became king, 25 December 1066](/wiki/Coronation_of_William_I "Coronation of William I"),[[47]](#cite_note-47) but three weeks since the surrender of English nobles and bishops at [Berkhampstead](/wiki/Berkhampstead "Berkhampstead"), allowing time to prepare a spectacular ceremony.[[46]](#cite_note-Strong_p38-46) Most of his successors were crowned within weeks, or even days, of their accession. [Edward I](/wiki/Edward_I_of_England "Edward I of England") was fighting in the [Ninth Crusade](/wiki/Ninth_Crusade "Ninth Crusade") when he acceded to the throne in 1272; he was crowned soon after his return in 1274.[[48]](#cite_note-48) [Edward II](/wiki/Edward_II "Edward II")'s coronation, similarly, was delayed by a campaign in [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") in 1307.[[49]](#cite_note-49) [Henry VI](/wiki/Henry_VI_of_England "Henry VI of England") was only a few months old when he acceded in 1422; he was crowned in 1429, but did not officially assume the reins of government until he was deemed of sufficient age, in 1437.[[50]](#cite_note-henry-vi-50) Pre-modern coronations were usually either on a Sunday, the [Christian Sabbath](/wiki/Christian_Sabbath "Christian Sabbath"), or on a [Christian holiday](/wiki/Christian_holiday "Christian holiday"). Edgar's coronation was at [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost"),[[51]](#cite_note-51) William I's on [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas") Day, possibly in imitation of the Byzantine emperors,[[52]](#cite_note-52) and [John](/wiki/John,_King_of_England "John, King of England")'s was on [Ascension Day](/wiki/Ascension_Day "Ascension Day").[[53]](#cite_note-53) Elizabeth I consulted her [astrologer](/wiki/Astrologer "Astrologer"), [John Dee](/wiki/John_Dee "John Dee"), before deciding on an auspicious date.[[54]](#cite_note-54) The coronations of Charles II in 1661 and Anne in 1702 were on [St George's Day](/wiki/St_George%27s_Day "St George's Day"), the feast of the [patron saint](/wiki/Patron_saint "Patron saint") of England.[[55]](#cite_note-Mason2006-55)

Under the Hanoverian monarchs in the late eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, it was deemed appropriate to extend the waiting period to several months, following a period of mourning for the previous monarch and to allow time for preparation of the ceremony.[[56]](#cite_note-royal.gov-coronation-56)
In the case of every monarch between [George IV](/wiki/George_IV "George IV") and [George V](/wiki/George_V "George V"), at least one year passed between accession and coronation.[[57]](#cite_note-uk-monarchs-list-57) [Edward VIII](/wiki/Edward_VIII "Edward VIII") was not crowned and his successor [George VI](/wiki/George_VI "George VI") was crowned 5 months after his accession. The coronation date of his predecessor had already been set; planning simply continued with a new monarch.[[58]](#cite_note-58) The [coronation of Charles III and Camilla](/wiki/Coronation_of_Charles_III_and_Camilla "Coronation of Charles III and Camilla") was held on 6 May 2023, eight months after he acceded to the throne.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

Since a period of time has often passed between accession and coronation, some monarchs were never crowned. [Edward V](/wiki/Edward_V "Edward V") and [Lady Jane Grey](/wiki/Lady_Jane_Grey "Lady Jane Grey") were both deposed before they could be crowned, in 1483 and 1553, respectively.[[60]](#cite_note-england-monarchs-list-60) [Edward VIII](/wiki/Edward_VIII "Edward VIII") also went uncrowned, as he abdicated in 1936 before the end of the customary one-year period between accession and coronation.[[57]](#cite_note-uk-monarchs-list-57) A monarch, however, accedes to the throne the moment their predecessor dies, not when they are crowned, hence the traditional proclamation: "[The king is dead, long live the king!](/wiki/The_king_is_dead,_long_live_the_king! "The king is dead, long live the king!")"[[61]](#cite_note-61)

### Location

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Location")]

The [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/History_of_Anglo-Saxon_England "History of Anglo-Saxon England") monarchs used various locations for their coronations, including [Bath](/wiki/Bath,_Somerset "Bath, Somerset"), [Kingston upon Thames](/wiki/Kingston_upon_Thames "Kingston upon Thames"), London, and [Winchester](/wiki/Winchester "Winchester"). The last Anglo-Saxon monarch, [Harold II](/wiki/Harold_Godwinson "Harold Godwinson"), was crowned at [Westminster Abbey](/wiki/Westminster_Abbey "Westminster Abbey") in 1066; the location was preserved for all future coronations.[[62]](#cite_note-62) When London was under the control of rebels,[[63]](#cite_note-63) [Henry III](/wiki/Henry_III_of_England "Henry III of England") was crowned at [Gloucester](/wiki/Gloucester "Gloucester") in 1216; he later chose to have a second coronation at Westminster in 1220.[[64]](#cite_note-64) Two hundred years later, Henry VI also had two coronations; as king of England in London in 1429, and as [king of France](/wiki/List_of_French_monarchs "List of French monarchs") in Paris in 1431.[[50]](#cite_note-henry-vi-50)

### Coronation of consorts and others

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Coronation of consorts and others")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Coronation_of_Henry_the_Young_King_-_Becket_Leaves_%28c.1220-1240%29%2C_f._3r_-_BL_Loan_MS_88.jpg/500px-Coronation_of_Henry_the_Young_King_-_Becket_Leaves_%28c.1220-1240%29%2C_f._3r_-_BL_Loan_MS_88.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coronation_of_Henry_the_Young_King_-_Becket_Leaves_(c.1220-1240),_f._3r_-_BL_Loan_MS_88.jpg)

The Coronation of [Henry the Young King](/wiki/Henry_the_Young_King "Henry the Young King") in 1170; he died young and did not become King of England

Coronations may be performed for a person other than the reigning monarch. In 1170, [Henry the Young King](/wiki/Henry_the_Young_King "Henry the Young King"), heir apparent to the throne, was crowned as a second king of England, subordinate to his father [Henry II](/wiki/Henry_II_of_England "Henry II of England");[[65]](#cite_note-65) such coronations were common practice in mediaeval France and Germany, but this is only one of two instances of its kind in England (the other being that of [Ecgfrith of Mercia](/wiki/Ecgfrith_of_Mercia "Ecgfrith of Mercia") in 796, crowned whilst his father, [Offa of Mercia](/wiki/Offa_of_Mercia "Offa of Mercia"), was still alive).[[66]](#cite_note-66) More commonly, a king's wife is crowned as [queen consort](/wiki/Queen_consort "Queen consort"). If the king is already married at the time of his coronation, a joint coronation of both king and queen may be performed.[[56]](#cite_note-royal.gov-coronation-56) The first such coronation was of [Henry II](/wiki/Henry_II_of_England "Henry II of England") and [Eleanor of Aquitaine](/wiki/Eleanor_of_Aquitaine "Eleanor of Aquitaine") in 1154; eighteen such coronations have been performed, including that of the co-rulers [William III](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England") and [Mary II](/wiki/Mary_II_of_England "Mary II of England").[[67]](#cite_note-67) The most recent was that of [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III") and his wife [Camilla](/wiki/Queen_Camilla_of_the_United_Kingdom "Queen Camilla of the United Kingdom") in 2023. If the king married, or remarried, after his coronation, or if his wife was not crowned with him for some other reason, she might be crowned in a separate ceremony. The first such separate coronation of a queen consort in England was that of [Matilda of Flanders](/wiki/Matilda_of_Flanders "Matilda of Flanders") in 1068;[[68]](#cite_note-68) the last was [Anne Boleyn](/wiki/Anne_Boleyn "Anne Boleyn")'s in 1533.[[69]](#cite_note-69) The most recent king to wed post-coronation, [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England"), did not have a separate coronation for his bride, [Catherine of Braganza](/wiki/Catherine_of_Braganza "Catherine of Braganza").[[70]](#cite_note-70) In some instances, the king's wife was simply unable to join him in the coronation ceremony due to circumstances preventing her from doing so. In 1821, George IV's estranged wife [Caroline of Brunswick](/wiki/Caroline_of_Brunswick "Caroline of Brunswick") was not invited to the ceremony; when she showed up at Westminster Abbey anyway, she was denied entry and turned away.[[71]](#cite_note-71) Following the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"), [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell") declined the crown but underwent a coronation in all but name in his second investiture as [Lord Protector](/wiki/Lord_Protector "Lord Protector") in 1657.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

## Participants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Participants")]

### Clergy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Clergy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Archbishops_of_Canterbury_and_York_1911.png/250px-Archbishops_of_Canterbury_and_York_1911.png)](/wiki/File:Archbishops_of_Canterbury_and_York_1911.png)

Archbishops of Canterbury and York at the coronation of King George V and Queen Mary in 1911

The Archbishop of Canterbury, who has precedence over all other clergy and all laypersons except members of the royal family,[[73]](#cite_note-precedence-73) traditionally officiates at coronations;[[74]](#cite_note-50-facts-74) in his absence, another bishop appointed by the monarch may take the archbishop's place.[[75]](#cite_note-oath-75) There have, however, been several exceptions. William I was crowned by the [Archbishop of York](/wiki/Archbishop_of_York "Archbishop of York"), since the Archbishop of Canterbury had been appointed by the [Antipope](/wiki/Antipope "Antipope") [Benedict X](/wiki/Benedict_X "Benedict X"), and this appointment was not recognised as valid by the Pope.[[76]](#cite_note-76) [Edward II](/wiki/Edward_II "Edward II") was crowned by the [Bishop of Winchester](/wiki/Bishop_of_Winchester "Bishop of Winchester") because the Archbishop of Canterbury had been exiled by [Edward I](/wiki/Edward_I_of_England "Edward I of England").[[77]](#cite_note-77) [Mary I](/wiki/Mary_I_of_England "Mary I of England"), a Catholic, refused to be crowned by the Protestant Archbishop [Thomas Cranmer](/wiki/Thomas_Cranmer "Thomas Cranmer"); the coronation was instead performed by the Bishop of Winchester.[[78]](#cite_note-78) [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I") was crowned by the [Bishop of Carlisle](/wiki/Bishop_of_Carlisle "Bishop of Carlisle") (to whose [see](/wiki/Episcopal_see "Episcopal see") is attached no special precedence) because the senior [prelates](/wiki/Prelate "Prelate") were "either dead, too old and infirm, unacceptable to the queen, or unwilling to serve".[[79]](#cite_note-79) Finally, when [James II](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England") was deposed and replaced with William III and Mary II jointly, the Archbishop of Canterbury refused to recognise the new sovereigns; he had to be replaced by the [Bishop of London](/wiki/Bishop_of_London "Bishop of London"), [Henry Compton](/wiki/Henry_Compton_(bishop) "Henry Compton (bishop)").[[80]](#cite_note-80) Hence, in almost all cases where the Archbishop of Canterbury has failed to participate, his place has been taken by a senior cleric: the Archbishop of York is second in precedence, the Bishop of London third, the Bishop of Durham fourth, and the Bishop of Winchester fifth.[[73]](#cite_note-precedence-73)

### Bishops Assistant

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Bishops Assistant")]

From the moment they enter the Abbey until the moment they leave, the monarch is flanked by two supporting bishops of the Church of England.

> The part played by two supporting bishops dates back to the coronation of [Edgar](/wiki/Edgar_of_England "Edgar of England") in 973: two bishops led him by hand into Bath Abbey. Since the coronation of [Richard I](/wiki/Richard_I_of_England "Richard I of England") in 1189, the Bishops of Bath & Wells and Durham have assumed this duty.  
> Custom has it that they accompany the monarch throughout the ceremony, flanking them as they process from the entrance of Westminster Abbey and standing either side of St Edward's Chair during the anointing. Bishops Assistant may also carry the Bible, paten, and chalice in the procession.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

The Bishop of Durham stands on the monarch's right and the Bishop of Bath and Wells on their left.[[82]](#cite_note-82)
During the Coronation of King [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III"), [Queen Camilla](/wiki/Queen_Camilla "Queen Camilla") was similarly accompanied by Bishops Assistant – the Bishops of Hereford and of Norwich – on her right and left respectively.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

### Great Officers of State

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Great Officers of State")]

The [Great Officers of State](/wiki/Great_Officer_of_State "Great Officer of State") traditionally participate during the ceremony. The offices of [Lord High Steward](/wiki/Lord_High_Steward "Lord High Steward") and [Lord High Constable](/wiki/Lord_High_Constable_of_England "Lord High Constable of England") have not been regularly filled since the 15th and 16th centuries respectively; they are, however, revived for coronation ceremonies.[[84]](#cite_note-lord-high-steward-84)[[85]](#cite_note-85) The [Lord Great Chamberlain](/wiki/Lord_Great_Chamberlain "Lord Great Chamberlain") enrobes the sovereign with the ceremonial vestments, with the aid of the [Groom of the Robes](/wiki/Groom_of_the_Robes "Groom of the Robes") and the [Master](/wiki/Master_of_the_Robes "Master of the Robes") (in the case of a king) or [Mistress](/wiki/Mistress_of_the_Robes "Mistress of the Robes") (in the case of a queen) of the Robes.[[43]](#cite_note-oremus-43)

The [Barons](/wiki/Warden_of_the_Cinque_Ports "Warden of the Cinque Ports") of the [Cinque Ports](/wiki/Cinque_Ports "Cinque Ports") also participated in the ceremony. Formerly, the barons were the members of the House of Commons representing the Cinque Ports of [Hastings](/wiki/Hastings_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Hastings (UK Parliament constituency)"), [New Romney](/wiki/New_Romney_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "New Romney (UK Parliament constituency)"), [Hythe](/wiki/Hythe_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Hythe (UK Parliament constituency)"), [Dover](/wiki/Dover_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Dover (UK Parliament constituency)") and [Sandwich](/wiki/Sandwich_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Sandwich (UK Parliament constituency)"). Reforms in the 19th century, however, integrated the Cinque Ports into a regular constituency system applied throughout the nation. At later coronations, barons were specially designated from among the city councillors for the specific purpose of attending coronations. Originally, the barons were charged with bearing a ceremonial canopy over the sovereign during the procession to and from Westminster Abbey. The last time the barons performed such a task was at the [coronation of George IV](/wiki/Coronation_of_George_IV "Coronation of George IV") in 1821. The barons did not return for the coronations of [William IV](/wiki/William_IV "William IV") (who insisted on a simpler, cheaper ceremonial) and [Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria"). At coronations since Victoria's, the barons have attended the ceremony, but they have not carried canopies.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

### Other claims to attend the coronation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Other claims to attend the coronation")]

See also: [Grand Serjeanty](/wiki/Serjeanty#Grand_serjeanty_vs_petty_serjeanty "Serjeanty")

Many landowners and other persons have honorific "duties" or privileges at the coronation. Such rights have traditionally been determined by a special [Court of Claims](/wiki/Court_of_Claims_(United_Kingdom) "Court of Claims (United Kingdom)"), over which the Lord High Steward traditionally presided. The first recorded Court of Claims was convened in 1377 for the coronation of [Richard II](/wiki/Richard_II_of_England "Richard II of England"). By the Tudor period, the hereditary post of Lord High Steward had merged with the Crown, and so [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII "Henry VIII") began the modern tradition of naming a temporary Steward for the coronation only, with separate commissioners to carry out the actual work of the court.[[84]](#cite_note-lord-high-steward-84)

In 1952, for example, the court accepted the claim of the [Dean of Westminster](/wiki/Dean_of_Westminster "Dean of Westminster") to advise the Queen on the proper procedure during the ceremony (for nearly a thousand years he and his predecessor abbots have kept an unpublished Red Book of practices), the claim of the [Lord Bishop of Durham](/wiki/Bishop_of_Durham "Bishop of Durham") and the [Lord Bishop of Bath and Wells](/wiki/Bishop_of_Bath_and_Wells "Bishop of Bath and Wells") to walk beside the Queen as she entered and exited the Abbey and to stand on either side of her through the entire coronation ritual, the claim of the [Earl of Shrewsbury](/wiki/Earl_of_Shrewsbury "Earl of Shrewsbury") in his capacity as [Lord High Steward of Ireland](/wiki/Lord_High_Steward_of_Ireland "Lord High Steward of Ireland") to carry a white staff. The legal claim of the [Scholars](/wiki/Queen%27s_Scholar "Queen's Scholar") of [Westminster School](/wiki/Westminster_School "Westminster School") to be the first to acclaim the monarch on behalf of the common people was formally disallowed by the court, but in practice their traditional shouts of "Vivat! Vivat Rex!" were still incorporated into the coronation anthem *[I was glad](/wiki/I_was_glad "I was glad")*.[[87]](#cite_note-87)

For the 2023 [coronation of Charles III and Camilla](/wiki/Coronation_of_Charles_III_and_Camilla "Coronation of Charles III and Camilla"), a Coronation Claims Office within the [Cabinet Office](/wiki/Cabinet_Office "Cabinet Office") was established instead of the court.[[88]](#cite_note-88)

### Other participants and guests

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Other participants and guests")]

Along with persons of nobility, the coronation ceremonies are also attended by a wide range of political figures, including the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom") and all members of the [Cabinet of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_United_Kingdom "Cabinet of the United Kingdom"), all [governors-general](/wiki/Governor-General "Governor-General") and prime ministers of the [Commonwealth realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm "Commonwealth realm"), all governors of British [Crown Colonies](/wiki/Crown_colony "Crown colony") (now British [Overseas Territories](/wiki/Overseas_Territories "Overseas Territories")), as well as the [heads of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") of dependent nations. Hereditary peers and their spouses are also invited. For Elizabeth II's coronation in 1953, 8,000 guests were squeezed into Westminster Abbey and each person had to make do with a maximum of 18 inches (46 cm) of seating.[[89]](#cite_note-89)

Dignitaries and representatives from other nations are also customarily invited.[[56]](#cite_note-royal.gov-coronation-56) Traditionally, foreign crowned monarchs and consorts did not attend the coronations of others and were instead represented by other royals. In 1953, [Prince Edward, Duke of Windsor (formerly Edward VIII)](/wiki/Edward_VIII "Edward VIII"), was not invited to the [coronation of Elizabeth II](/wiki/Coronation_of_Elizabeth_II "Coronation of Elizabeth II"), with the reason given that it was contrary to precedent for a sovereign or former sovereign to attend any coronation of another.[[90]](#cite_note-90) The [coronation of Charles III and Camilla](/wiki/Coronation_of_Charles_III_and_Camilla "Coronation of Charles III and Camilla") in 2023 broke with that precedent and 16 foreign monarchs attended.[[91]](#cite_note-91)[[92]](#cite_note-92) English and British queens dowager also did not traditionally attend coronations until [Queen Mary](/wiki/Mary_of_Teck "Mary of Teck") broke precedent by attending the 1937 [coronation of her son, George VI](/wiki/Coronation_of_George_VI_and_Elizabeth "Coronation of George VI and Elizabeth").[[93]](#cite_note-93)

## Service

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Service")]

The general framework of the coronation service is based on the sections contained in the Second Recension used in 973 for King Edgar. Although the service has undergone two major revisions and a translation, and has been modified for each coronation for the following thousand years, the sequence of taking an oath, anointing, investing of regalia, crowning and enthronement found in the Anglo-Saxon text[[94]](#cite_note-94) have remained constant.[[95]](#cite_note-95) The coronation ceremonies takes place within the framework of [Holy Communion](/wiki/Holy_Communion "Holy Communion").[[96]](#cite_note-96)

### Recognition and oath

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Recognition and oath")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Edward_VII_coronation_oath_1902.jpg/250px-Edward_VII_coronation_oath_1902.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edward_VII_coronation_oath_1902.jpg)

[Edward VII](/wiki/Edward_VII "Edward VII") taking the oath in 1902

Main article: [Coronation oath of the British monarch](/wiki/Coronation_oath_of_the_British_monarch "Coronation oath of the British monarch")

Before the entrance of the sovereign, the litany of the saints is sung during the procession of the clergy and other dignitaries. For the entrance of the monarch, an anthem from [Psalm 122](/wiki/Psalm_122 "Psalm 122"), *[I was glad](/wiki/I_was_glad "I was glad")*, is sung.[[97]](#cite_note-97)

The sovereign enters Westminster Abbey wearing the *crimson surcoat* and the *Robe of State of crimson velvet* and takes their seat on a Chair of Estate. [Garter Principal King of Arms](/wiki/Garter_King_of_Arms "Garter King of Arms"), the Archbishop of Canterbury, the [Lord Chancellor](/wiki/Lord_Chancellor "Lord Chancellor"), the [Lord Great Chamberlain](/wiki/Lord_Great_Chamberlain "Lord Great Chamberlain"), the [Lord High Constable](/wiki/Lord_High_Constable_of_England "Lord High Constable of England") and the [Earl Marshal](/wiki/Earl_Marshal "Earl Marshal") go to the east, south, west and north of the coronation theatre.[[98]](#cite_note-98) At each side, the archbishop calls for the recognition of the sovereign, with the words:

> Sirs, I here present unto you [name], your undoubted King/Queen. Wherefore all you who are come this day to do your homage and service, are you willing to do the same?[[43]](#cite_note-oremus-43)

After the people acclaim the sovereign at each side, the archbishop administers an oath to the sovereign.[[43]](#cite_note-oremus-43) Since the [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution"), the [Coronation Oath Act 1688](/wiki/Coronation_Oath_Act_1688 "Coronation Oath Act 1688") has required, among other things, that the sovereign "Promise and Sweare to Governe the People of this Kingdome of England and the Dominions thereto belonging according to the Statutes in Parlyament Agreed on and the Laws and Customs of the same".[[99]](#cite_note-99) The oath has been modified without statutory authority; for example, at the [coronation of Elizabeth II](/wiki/Coronation_of_Elizabeth_II "Coronation of Elizabeth II"), the exchange between the Queen and the archbishop was as follows:[[43]](#cite_note-oremus-43)

> *The Archbishop of Canterbury:* Will you solemnly promise and swear to govern the Peoples of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, the [Union of South Africa](/wiki/Union_of_South_Africa "Union of South Africa"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Dominion_of_Pakistan "Dominion of Pakistan") and [Ceylon](/wiki/Dominion_of_Ceylon "Dominion of Ceylon"), and of your Possessions and other Territories to any of them belonging or pertaining, according to their respective laws and customs?
>
> *The Queen:* I solemnly promise so to do.
>
> *The Archbishop of Canterbury:* Will you to your power cause Law and Justice, in Mercy, to be executed in all your judgments?
>
> *The Queen:* I will.
>
> *The Archbishop of Canterbury:* Will you to the utmost of your power maintain the Laws of God and the true profession of the Gospel? Will you to the utmost of your power maintain in the United Kingdom the Protestant Reformed Religion established by law? Will you maintain and preserve inviolable the settlement of the Church of England, and the doctrine, worship, discipline, and government thereof, as by law established in England? And will you preserve unto the Bishops and Clergy of England, and to the Churches there committed to their charge, all such rights and privileges, as by law do or shall appertain to them or any of them?
>
> *The Queen:* All this I promise to do. The things which I have here before promised, I will perform, and keep. So help me God.[[43]](#cite_note-oremus-43)

In addition to the oath, the monarch may take what is known as the [Accession Declaration](/wiki/Accession_Declaration "Accession Declaration") if they have not yet made it. This declaration was first required by the [Bill of Rights 1689](/wiki/Bill_of_Rights_1689 "Bill of Rights 1689") and is required to be taken at either the first meeting of the parliament after a new monarch's accession (i.e. during the [State Opening of Parliament](/wiki/State_Opening_of_Parliament "State Opening of Parliament")) or at their coronation. The monarch additionally swears a separate oath to preserve [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism") church government in the [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland") and this oath is taken before the coronation.[[75]](#cite_note-oath-75)

Once the taking of the oath concludes, an ecclesiastic presents a Bible to the sovereign, saying "Here is Wisdom; This is the royal Law; These are the lively Oracles of God."[[43]](#cite_note-oremus-43) The Bible used is a full [King James Bible](/wiki/King_James_Bible "King James Bible"), including the [Apocrypha](/wiki/Biblical_apocrypha#King_James_Version "Biblical apocrypha").[[100]](#cite_note-100) At Elizabeth II's coronation, the Bible was presented by the [Moderator of the General Assembly](/wiki/Moderator_of_the_General_Assembly_of_the_Church_of_Scotland "Moderator of the General Assembly of the Church of Scotland") of the [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland"). Once the Bible is presented, the [Holy Communion](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist") is celebrated, with a special Collect for the coronation, but the service is interrupted after the [Nicene Creed](/wiki/Nicene_Creed "Nicene Creed"). At the coronation of Elizabeth II, the Epistle was [1 Peter 2:13–17](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/1_Peter#2:13 "s:Bible (King James)/1 Peter"), which instructs readers to respect and obey civil government, and the Gospel was [Matthew 22:15–22](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Matthew#22:15 "s:Bible (King James)/Matthew"), which contains Jesus's famous instruction to "render unto Caesar the things that are Caesar's".[[43]](#cite_note-oremus-43)

### Anointing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Anointing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Spoon_and_ampulla.jpg/330px-Spoon_and_ampulla.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spoon_and_ampulla.jpg)

The [Ampulla](/wiki/Crown_Jewels_of_the_United_Kingdom#Ampulla "Crown Jewels of the United Kingdom") and [Coronation spoon](/wiki/Crown_Jewels_of_the_United_Kingdom#Spoon "Crown Jewels of the United Kingdom") used in the anointing

After the Communion service is interrupted, the anthem *[Come, Holy Ghost](/wiki/Veni_Creator_Spiritus "Veni Creator Spiritus")* is recited, as a prelude to the act of anointing. After this anthem, the Archbishop recites a prayer in preparation for the anointing, which is based on the ancient prayer *Deus electorum fortitudo* also used in the anointing of French kings. After this prayer, the coronation anthem *Zadok the Priest* (by George Frederick Handel) is sung by the choir; meanwhile, the *crimson robe* is removed, and the sovereign proceeds to the [Coronation Chair](/wiki/Coronation_Chair "Coronation Chair") for the anointing,[[43]](#cite_note-oremus-43) which has been set in a prominent position, wearing the *anointing gown*. In 1953, the chair stood atop a dais of several steps.[[101]](#cite_note-101) This mediaeval chair has a cavity in the base into which the [Stone of Scone](/wiki/Stone_of_Scone "Stone of Scone") is fitted for the ceremony. Also known as the "Stone of Destiny", it was used for ancient Scottish coronations until brought to England by [Edward I](/wiki/Edward_I_of_England "Edward I of England"). It has been used for every coronation at Westminster Abbey since. Until 1996, the stone was kept with the chair in Westminster Abbey, but it was moved that year to [Edinburgh Castle](/wiki/Edinburgh_Castle "Edinburgh Castle") in Scotland, where it is displayed on the proviso that it be returned to Westminster Abbey for use at future coronations.[[10]](#cite_note-abbey-10) It was announced by the [First Minister](/wiki/First_Minister_of_Scotland "First Minister of Scotland") in 2020 that the Stone will be relocated to [Perth City Hall](/wiki/Perth_City_Hall "Perth City Hall") in 2024.[[102]](#cite_note-102)

Once seated in this chair, a [canopy](/wiki/Baldachin "Baldachin") of golden cloth was in the past held over the monarch's head for the [anointing](/wiki/Anointing "Anointing"). The duty of acting as canopy-bearers was performed in recent coronations by four [Knights of the Garter](/wiki/Order_of_the_Garter "Order of the Garter").[[43]](#cite_note-oremus-43) This element of the coronation service is considered sacred and is concealed from public gaze;[[103]](#cite_note-103) it has never been photographed or televised. The Dean of Westminster pours [consecrated oil](/wiki/Consecrated_oil "Consecrated oil") from an eagle-shaped [ampulla](/wiki/Ampulla "Ampulla") into a [filigreed](/wiki/Filigree "Filigree") spoon with which the Archbishop of Canterbury anoints the sovereign in the form of a cross on the hands, head, and heart.[[43]](#cite_note-oremus-43) The [Coronation Spoon](/wiki/Coronation_Spoon "Coronation Spoon") is the only part of the medieval Crown Jewels which survived the [Commonwealth of England](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_England "Commonwealth of England").[[104]](#cite_note-crown-jewels-104) While performing the anointing, the Archbishop recites a consecratory formula recalling the anointing of King [Solomon](/wiki/Solomon "Solomon") by [Nathan the prophet](/wiki/Nathan_(prophet) "Nathan (prophet)") and [Zadok](/wiki/Zadok "Zadok") the priest.[[43]](#cite_note-oremus-43)

After being anointed, the monarch rises from the Coronation Chair and kneels down at a [faldstool](/wiki/Faldstool "Faldstool") placed in front of it. The archbishop then concludes the ceremonies of the anointing by reciting a prayer that is a modified English translation of the ancient Latin prayer *Deus, Dei Filius*, which dates back to the Anglo-Saxon second recension.[[105]](#cite_note-105) Once this prayer is finished, the monarch rises and sits again in the Coronation Chair. At this point in 2023 the screen was removed.[[43]](#cite_note-oremus-43)

### Investing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Investing")]

The sovereign is then enrobed in the *colobium sindonis* (shroud tunic), over which is placed the *supertunica*.[[43]](#cite_note-oremus-43)

The Lord Great Chamberlain presents the [spurs](/wiki/Spur "Spur"),[[43]](#cite_note-oremus-43) which represent [chivalry](/wiki/Chivalry "Chivalry").[[104]](#cite_note-crown-jewels-104) The Archbishop of Canterbury, assisted by other bishops, then presents the Sword of State to the sovereign, who places it on the altar. The sovereign is then further robed, this time receiving bracelets and putting the *Robe Royal* and *Stole Royal* on top of the *supertunica*. The Archbishop then delivers several [Crown Jewels](/wiki/Crown_Jewels_of_the_United_Kingdom "Crown Jewels of the United Kingdom") to the sovereign. First, he delivers the [Orb](/wiki/Sovereign%27s_Orb "Sovereign's Orb"),[[43]](#cite_note-oremus-43) a hollow gold sphere decorated with precious and semi-precious stones. The Orb is surmounted by a cross, representing the dominion of the divine over the world;[[106]](#cite_note-106) it is returned to the altar immediately after being received.[[43]](#cite_note-oremus-43) Next, the sovereign receives a ring representing their "marriage" to their territories, their subjects, and the divine.[[107]](#cite_note-107) The [Sovereign's Sceptre with Dove](/wiki/Sovereign%27s_Sceptre_with_Dove "Sovereign's Sceptre with Dove"), so called because it is surmounted by a dove representing the [Holy Ghost](/wiki/Holy_Ghost "Holy Ghost"), and the [Sovereign's Sceptre with Cross](/wiki/Sovereign%27s_Sceptre_with_Cross "Sovereign's Sceptre with Cross"), which incorporates [Cullinan I](/wiki/Cullinan_Diamond "Cullinan Diamond"), are delivered to the sovereign.[[108]](#cite_note-108)

### Crowning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Crowning")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Crowning_of_George_VI.jpg/330px-Crowning_of_George_VI.jpg)](/wiki/File:Crowning_of_George_VI.jpg)

Crowning of King [George VI](/wiki/George_VI "George VI") in 1937, by [Henry Charles Brewer](/wiki/Henry_Charles_Brewer "Henry Charles Brewer")

The Archbishop of Canterbury lifts [St Edward's Crown](/wiki/St_Edward%27s_Crown "St Edward's Crown") from the high altar, sets it back down, and says a prayer: "Oh God, the crown of the faithful; bless we beseech thee and sanctify this thy servant our king/queen, and as thou dost this day set a crown of pure gold upon his/her head, so enrich his/her royal heart with thine abundant grace, and crown him/her with all princely virtues through the King Eternal Jesus Christ our Lord. Amen". This prayer is the translation of the ancient formula *Deus tuorum Corona fidelium*, which first appeared in the twelfth-century third recension.[[109]](#cite_note-109)

The [Dean of Westminster](/wiki/Dean_of_Westminster "Dean of Westminster") picks up the crown and he, the archbishop and several other high-ranking bishops proceed to the Coronation Chair where the crown is handed back to the archbishop, who reverently places it on the monarch's head.[[110]](#cite_note-110) At this moment, the king or queen is crowned, and the guests in the abbey cry in unison three times, "God Save the King/Queen". The trumpeters sound a fanfare and church bells ring out across the kingdom, as [gun salutes](/wiki/Gun_salute "Gun salute") echo from the [Tower of London](/wiki/Tower_of_London "Tower of London") and [Hyde Park](/wiki/Hyde_Park,_London "Hyde Park, London").[[111]](#cite_note-111)

Finally, the archbishop, standing before the monarch, says the crowning formula, which is a translation of the ancient Latin prayer *Coronet te Deus*: "God crown you with a crown of glory and righteousness, that having a right faith and manifold fruit of good works, you may obtain the crown of an everlasting kingdom by the gift of him whose kingdom endureth for ever." To this the guests, with heads bowed, say "[Amen](/wiki/Amen "Amen")".[[112]](#cite_note-Butler1989-112)

When this prayer is finished, the choir sings an English translation of the traditional Latin [antiphon](/wiki/Antiphon "Antiphon") *Confortare*: "Be strong and of a good courage; keep the commandments of the Lord thy God, and walk in his ways". During the singing of this antiphon, all stand in their places, and the monarch remains seated in the Coronation Chair still wearing the crown and holding the sceptres. The recitation of this antiphon is followed by a rite of [benediction](/wiki/Benediction "Benediction") consisting of several prayers, after each one the congregation replies with "a loud and hearty Amen".[[43]](#cite_note-oremus-43)

### Enthronement and homage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Enthronement and homage")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Elizabeth_I_in_coronation_robes.jpg/330px-Elizabeth_I_in_coronation_robes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Elizabeth_I_in_coronation_robes.jpg)

[Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I") wearing the crown and holding the sceptre and orb at the end of her coronation.

The benediction being concluded, the sovereign rises from the Coronation Chair and is borne into a throne. Once the monarch is seated on the throne, the formula *Stand firm, and hold fast from henceforth...* is recited;[[43]](#cite_note-oremus-43) a translation of the Latin formula *Sta et retine...*, which was first used in England in the tenth-century second recension, and also appeared in French, German and imperial coronation texts.[[113]](#cite_note-113)

After the enthronement proper, the act of homage takes place: the archbishops and bishops swear their [fealty](/wiki/Fealty "Fealty"), saying "I, N., Archbishop [Bishop] of N., will be faithful and true, and faith and truth will bear unto you, our Sovereign Lord [Lady], King [Queen] of this Realm and Defender of the Faith, and unto your heirs and successors according to law. So help me God." In the past peers then proceeded to pay their homage, saying "I, N., Duke [Marquess, Earl, Viscount, Baron or Lord] of N., do become your liege man of life and limb, and of earthly worship; and faith and truth will I bear unto you, to live and die, against all manner of folks. So help me God."[[43]](#cite_note-oremus-43) The clergy pay homage together, led by the Archbishop of Canterbury. Next, members of the royal family pay homage individually. The peers were then led by the premier peers of their rank: the dukes by the premier duke, the marquesses by the premier marquess, and so forth.[[43]](#cite_note-oremus-43) In the shortened [coronation of Charles III and Camilla](/wiki/Coronation_of_Charles_III_and_Camilla "Coronation of Charles III and Camilla"), the paying of homage by the peerage was omitted.[[114]](#cite_note-114)

If there is a [queen consort](/wiki/Queen_consort "Queen consort"), she is anointed, invested, crowned and enthroned in a simple ceremony immediately after homage is paid. The Communion service interrupted earlier is resumed and completed, but with special prayers: there are prayers for the monarch and consort at the Offertory and a special preface.[[56]](#cite_note-royal.gov-coronation-56)[[43]](#cite_note-oremus-43) Finally, the monarch and consort receive Communion, the *[Gloria in excelsis Deo](/wiki/Gloria_in_excelsis_Deo "Gloria in excelsis Deo")* is sung and the [blessing](/wiki/Dismissal_(liturgy) "Dismissal (liturgy)") is given.[[115]](#cite_note-115)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Mounted_Band_of_The_Scots_Greys%2C_Coronation_Parade%2C_1937_by_Harry_Greville_Wood_Irwin.jpg/330px-Mounted_Band_of_The_Scots_Greys%2C_Coronation_Parade%2C_1937_by_Harry_Greville_Wood_Irwin.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mounted_Band_of_The_Scots_Greys,_Coronation_Parade,_1937_by_Harry_Greville_Wood_Irwin.jpg)

*Mounted Band of The Scots Greys, Coronation Parade, 1937* by Harry Greville Wood Irwin. Painted in 1937, depicting the Coronation of King [George VI](/wiki/George_VI "George VI")

### Closing procession

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Closing procession")]

The sovereign then exits the coronation theatre, entering St Edward's Chapel (within the abbey), preceded by the bearers of the Sword of State, the Sword of Spiritual Justice, the Sword of Temporal Justice and the blunt [Sword of Mercy](/wiki/Sword_of_Mercy "Sword of Mercy").[[116]](#cite_note-116) While the monarch is in St. Edward's chapel, the choir recites an English translation of the hymn of thanksgiving *Te Deum laudamus*. St Edward's Crown and all the other regalia are laid on the High Altar of the chapel;[[43]](#cite_note-oremus-43) the sovereign removes the *Robe Royal* and *Stole Royal*, exchanges the *crimson surcoat* for the *purple surcoat*[[117]](#cite_note-cox-sovereigns-robes-117) and is enrobed in the *Imperial Robe of purple velvet*. The sovereign then dons the [Imperial State Crown](/wiki/Imperial_State_Crown "Imperial State Crown") and takes into their hands the Sceptre with the Cross and the Orb and leaves the chapel first while all present sing the [national anthem](/wiki/God_Save_the_King "God Save the King").[[43]](#cite_note-oremus-43)

## Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Music")]

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg.png)

[Zadok the Priest](/wiki/File:Handel_-_Zadok_the_Priest,_HWV_258_(St_Matthew%27s_Concert_Choir,_Giromella).oga "File:Handel - Zadok the Priest, HWV 258 (St Matthew's Concert Choir, Giromella).oga")

Handel's *[Zadok the Priest](/wiki/Zadok_the_Priest "Zadok the Priest")* (1727), performed by St Matthew's Choir, 2013

---

*Problems playing this file? See [media help](/wiki/Help:Media "Help:Media").*

The music played at coronations has been primarily classical and religiously inspired. Much of the [choral music](/wiki/Choral_music "Choral music") uses texts from the Bible which have been used at coronations since King Edgar's coronation at Bath in 973 and are known as [coronation anthems](/wiki/Coronation_anthem "Coronation anthem"). In the coronations following the [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation_in_England "Reformation in England"), court musicians, often the [Master of the King's Music](/wiki/Master_of_the_King%27s_Music "Master of the King's Music"), were commissioned to compose new [settings](/wiki/Musical_setting "Musical setting") for the traditional texts. The most frequently used piece is *[Zadok the Priest](/wiki/Zadok_the_Priest "Zadok the Priest")* by [George Frideric Handel](/wiki/George_Frideric_Handel "George Frideric Handel"); one of four anthems commissioned from him for [George II](/wiki/George_II_of_Great_Britain "George II of Great Britain")'s coronation in 1727. It has featured in every coronation since, an achievement unparalleled by any other piece. Previous settings of the same text were composed by [Henry Lawes](/wiki/Henry_Lawes "Henry Lawes") for the 1661 coronation of [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_Great_Britain "Charles II of Great Britain") and [Thomas Tomkins](/wiki/Thomas_Tomkins "Thomas Tomkins") for [Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_Great_Britain "Charles I of Great Britain") in 1621.[[118]](#cite_note-118)

In the 19th century, works by major European composers were often used, but when Sir [Frederick Bridge](/wiki/Frederick_Bridge "Frederick Bridge") was appointed director of music for the 1902 [coronation of Edward VII](/wiki/Coronation_of_King_Edward_VII_and_Queen_Alexandra "Coronation of King Edward VII and Queen Alexandra"), he decided that it ought to be a celebration of four hundred years of British music. Compositions by [Thomas Tallis](/wiki/Thomas_Tallis "Thomas Tallis"), [Orlando Gibbons](/wiki/Orlando_Gibbons "Orlando Gibbons") and [Henry Purcell](/wiki/Henry_Purcell "Henry Purcell") were included alongside works by contemporary composers such as [Arthur Sullivan](/wiki/Arthur_Sullivan "Arthur Sullivan"), [Charles Villiers Stanford](/wiki/Charles_Villiers_Stanford "Charles Villiers Stanford") and [John Stainer](/wiki/John_Stainer "John Stainer").[[119]](#cite_note-119) [Hubert Parry](/wiki/Hubert_Parry "Hubert Parry")'s *[I was glad](/wiki/I_was_glad "I was glad")* was written as the entrance anthem for the 1902 coronation, replacing an 1831 setting by [Thomas Attwood](/wiki/Thomas_Attwood_(composer) "Thomas Attwood (composer)"); it contains a bridge section partway through so that the scholars of [Westminster School](/wiki/Westminster_School "Westminster School") can exercise their right to be the first commoners to acclaim the sovereign, shouting their traditional *"[vivats](/wiki/Vive,_Viva "Vive, Viva")"* as the sovereign enters the coronation theatre. This anthem and [Charles Villiers Stanford](/wiki/Charles_Villiers_Stanford "Charles Villiers Stanford")'s *[Gloria in excelsis](/wiki/Gloria_in_excelsis_Deo "Gloria in excelsis Deo")* (1911) have also been used regularly in recent coronations, as has the national anthem, *[God Save the King](/wiki/God_Save_the_King "God Save the King")* (or Queen).[[120]](#cite_note-120) Other composers whose music featured in Elizabeth II's coronation include [Sir George Dyson](/wiki/George_Dyson_(composer) "George Dyson (composer)"), [Gordon Jacob](/wiki/Gordon_Jacob "Gordon Jacob"), [Sir William Henry Harris](/wiki/William_Henry_Harris "William Henry Harris"), [Herbert Howells](/wiki/Herbert_Howells "Herbert Howells"), [Sir William Walton](/wiki/William_Walton "William Walton"), [Samuel Sebastian Wesley](/wiki/Samuel_Sebastian_Wesley "Samuel Sebastian Wesley"), [Ralph Vaughan Williams](/wiki/Ralph_Vaughan_Williams "Ralph Vaughan Williams") and the Canadian-resident but English-born [Healey Willan](/wiki/Healey_Willan "Healey Willan").[[121]](#cite_note-121) Ralph Vaughan Williams suggested that a congregational hymn be included. This was approved by the Queen and the Archbishop of Canterbury, so Vaughan Williams recast his 1928 arrangement of *[Old 100th](/wiki/Old_100th "Old 100th")*, the English [metrical](/wiki/Metrical_Psalm "Metrical Psalm") version of [Psalm 100](/wiki/Psalm_100 "Psalm 100"), the *Jubilate Deo* ("All people that on earth do dwell") for congregation, organ and orchestra: the setting has become ubiquitous at festal occasions in the Anglophone world.[[122]](#cite_note-122) At the 2023 coronation, new compositions were by [Roxanna Panufnik](/wiki/Roxanna_Panufnik "Roxanna Panufnik"), [Tarik O'Regan](/wiki/Tarik_O%27Regan "Tarik O'Regan"), [Andrew Lloyd Webber](/wiki/Andrew_Lloyd_Webber "Andrew Lloyd Webber") and [Debbie Wiseman](/wiki/Debbie_Wiseman "Debbie Wiseman"). Other innovations included a piece performed by a [Gospel choir](/wiki/Gospel_music "Gospel music") and a psalm sung in [Byzantine chant](/wiki/Byzantine_chant "Byzantine chant").[[123]](#cite_note-Music-details-123)[[124]](#cite_note-Music-RF-124)

## Dress

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Dress")]

Several participants in the ceremony wear special costumes, uniforms or robes. For those in attendance (other than members of the royal family) what to wear is laid down in detail by the Earl Marshal prior to each Coronation and published in *[The London Gazette](/wiki/The_London_Gazette "The London Gazette")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Sovereign's robes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Sovereign's robes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/George_IV_coronation.jpg/330px-George_IV_coronation.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_IV_coronation.jpg)

[George IV](/wiki/George_IV "George IV")'s long train was borne by the Master of the Robes and eight eldest sons of peers. The king (left) found the enormous weight of the robes very inconvenient.[[125]](#cite_note-125)

The sovereign wears a variety of robes and other garments during the course of the ceremony. In contrast to the history and tradition which surround the [regalia](/wiki/Crown_Jewels_of_the_United_Kingdom "Crown Jewels of the United Kingdom"), it has been customary for most coronation robes to be newly made for each monarch.[[126]](#cite_note-126) However, at the 2023 coronation, vestments were largely reused from previous coronations instead.[[127]](#cite_note-127)

**Worn for the first part of the service (and the processions beforehand):**

* *Crimson surcoat* – the regular dress during most of the ceremony, worn under all other robes. In 1953, Elizabeth II wore a newly made gown in place of a surcoat.[[117]](#cite_note-cox-sovereigns-robes-117)
* *Robe of State of crimson velvet* or *Parliament Robe* – the first robe used at a coronation, worn on entry to the abbey and later at [State Openings of Parliament](/wiki/State_Opening_of_Parliament "State Opening of Parliament"). It consists of an ermine cape and a long crimson velvet train lined with further ermine and decorated with gold lace.[[117]](#cite_note-cox-sovereigns-robes-117)

**Worn over the surcoat for the Anointing:**

* *Anointing gown* – a simple and austere garment worn during the anointing. It is plain white, bears no decoration and fastens at the back.[[117]](#cite_note-cox-sovereigns-robes-117)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/A_Circumstantial_Account_of_the_Preparations_for_the_Coronation_of_His_Majesty_King_Charles_the_Second_1820_h.jpg/250px-A_Circumstantial_Account_of_the_Preparations_for_the_Coronation_of_His_Majesty_King_Charles_the_Second_1820_h.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_Circumstantial_Account_of_the_Preparations_for_the_Coronation_of_His_Majesty_King_Charles_the_Second_1820_h.jpg)

The *Colobium sindonis*, *Supertunica* and *Stole Royal* used in the 1661 coronation of [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England")

**Robes with which the Sovereign is invested (worn thereafter until Communion):**

* *[Colobium sindonis](/wiki/Colobium_sindonis "Colobium sindonis")* ("shroud tunic") – the first robe with which the sovereign is invested. It is a loose white undergarment of fine linen cloth edged with a lace border, open at the sides, sleeveless and cut low at the neck. It symbolises the derivation of royal authority from the people.[[117]](#cite_note-cox-sovereigns-robes-117)
* *Supertunica* – the second robe with which the sovereign is invested. It is a long coat of gold silk which reaches to the ankles and has wide-flowing sleeves. It is lined with rose-coloured silk, trimmed with gold lace, woven with national symbols and fastened by a sword belt. It derives from the full dress uniform of a consul of the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire").[[117]](#cite_note-cox-sovereigns-robes-117)
* *Robe Royal* or *Pallium Regale* – the main robe worn during the ceremony and used during the crowning.[[43]](#cite_note-oremus-43) It is a four-square mantle, lined in crimson silk and decorated with silver coronets, national symbols and silver imperial eagles in the four corners. It is lay, rather than liturgical, in nature.[[117]](#cite_note-cox-sovereigns-robes-117)
