* *Stole Royal* or *armilla* – a gold silk [stole](/wiki/Stole_(vestment) "Stole (vestment)") or scarf which accompanies the Robe Royal, richly and heavily embroidered with gold and silver thread, set with jewels and lined with rose-coloured silk and gold fringing.[[117]](#cite_note-cox-sovereigns-robes-117)

**Worn for the final part of the service (and the processions which follow):**

* *Purple surcoat* – the counterpart to the crimson surcoat, worn during the final part of the ceremony.[[117]](#cite_note-cox-sovereigns-robes-117)
* *Imperial Robe of purple velvet* – the robe worn at the conclusion of the ceremony, on exit from the abbey. It comprises an embroidered ermine cape with a train of purple silk velvet, trimmed with Canadian ermine and fully lined with pure silk English satin. The purple recalls the imperial robes of [Roman Emperors](/wiki/Roman_Emperor "Roman Emperor").[[117]](#cite_note-cox-sovereigns-robes-117)

#### Headwear

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Headwear")]

Male sovereigns up to and including [George VI](/wiki/George_VI "George VI") have traditionally worn a crimson [cap of maintenance](/wiki/Cap_of_maintenance "Cap of maintenance") for the opening procession and when seated in the Chair of Estate during the first part of the service. [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III") arrived at his coronation bareheaded in 2023, rather than with the cap. Female sovereigns (and some female consorts) have traditionally worn the [George IV State Diadem](/wiki/George_IV_State_Diadem "George IV State Diadem"), first worn by its namesake, [George IV](/wiki/George_IV "George IV"). For the Anointing, the sovereign is bareheaded, and remains so until the Crowning. Monarchs are usually crowned with [St Edward's Crown](/wiki/St_Edward%27s_Crown "St Edward's Crown") but some have chosen to use other crowns as it weighs 2.23 kg (4.9 lb). For the final part of the service, and the processions that follow, it is exchanged for the lighter [Imperial State Crown](/wiki/Imperial_State_Crown "Imperial State Crown").[[128]](#cite_note-128)

### Other members of the royal family

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Other members of the royal family")]

Certain other members of the royal family wear distinctive robes, most particularly [queens consort](/wiki/Queens_consort "Queens consort") (including [dowagers](/wiki/Dowagers "Dowagers")) and [princesses of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Princess_of_the_United_Kingdom "Princess of the United Kingdom"), all of whom wear purple velvet [mantles](/wiki/Mantle_(clothing) "Mantle (clothing)") edged with [ermine](/wiki/Stoat "Stoat") over their court dresses. Other members of the royal family in attendance dress according to the conventions listed below, except that [royal dukes](/wiki/Royal_duke "Royal duke") wear a distinctive form of peer's robe, which has six rows of ermine on the cape and additional ermine on miniver edging to the front of the robe.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Headwear

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Headwear")]

Queens consort in the 20th century arrived at their coronation bareheaded, and remained so until the point in the service when they were crowned with their own [crown](/wiki/Consort_crown "Consort crown"). In the late 17th century and 18th century, queens consort wore [Mary of Modena's State Diadem](/wiki/Mary_of_Modena%27s_State_Crown "Mary of Modena's State Crown").[[129]](#cite_note-129) Prior to the 20th century it was not usual for [dowager queens](/wiki/Dowager_queens "Dowager queens") to attend coronations, but [Queen Mary](/wiki/Mary_of_Teck "Mary of Teck") and [Queen Elizabeth The Queen Mother](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_The_Queen_Mother "Queen Elizabeth The Queen Mother") both attended the coronations of George VI and Elizabeth II respectively, and each wore the crown, minus its arches, with which she had been crowned for the duration of the service.[[130]](#cite_note-130)[[131]](#cite_note-131)

Traditionally, princesses and [princes of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Prince_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prince of the United Kingdom") were provided with distinctive forms of [coronet](/wiki/Coronet "Coronet"), which they wore during the service. A male heir-apparent's coronet displays four crosses-pattée alternating with four fleurs-de-lis, surmounted by an arch. The same style, without the arch, is used by other children and siblings of the monarch. The coronets of children of the heir-apparent display four fleurs-de-lis, two crosses-pattée and two strawberry leaves. A fourth style, including four crosses-pattée and four strawberry leaves, is used for the children of the sons and brothers of sovereigns. The tradition of coronets was abolished for the 2023 coronation, and members of the royal family dressed in robes of one of their [orders of chivalry](/wiki/Orders_of_chivalry "Orders of chivalry").[[132]](#cite_note-132)

### Peers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Peers")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Earl_coronation_robes.jpg/250px-Earl_coronation_robes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Earl_coronation_robes.jpg)

An earl's coronation robes

All peers and peeresses in attendance are "expected to wear" Robes of State, as described below.[[133]](#cite_note-Gazette1-133) These robes are different to the [Parliament Robe](/wiki/Robes_of_the_British_peerage#Parliament_robes "Robes of the British peerage") (worn on occasion by peers who are members of the House of Lords); all peers summoned to attend wear the Robe of State, regardless of membership of the House of Lords, and peeresses' robes are worn not only by women who are peers in their own right, but also by wives and widows of peers.[[134]](#cite_note-CoxRobesPeerage-134)[[135]](#cite_note-135) Those entitled to a [collar](/wiki/Collar_(order) "Collar (order)") of an [order of knighthood](/wiki/Order_of_knighthood "Order of knighthood") wear it over (and attached to) the cape.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Peers' robes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Peers' robes")]

A peer's [coronation robe](/wiki/Robes_of_the_British_peerage#Coronation_robes "Robes of the British peerage") is a full-length [cloak](/wiki/Cloak "Cloak")-type garment of crimson velvet, edged down the front with [miniver pure](/wiki/Miniver "Miniver"), with a full [cape](/wiki/Cape "Cape") (also of miniver pure) attached. On the cape, rows of "ermine tails (or the like)"[[133]](#cite_note-Gazette1-133) indicate the peer's rank: dukes have four rows, marquesses three and a half, earls three, viscounts two and a half, and barons and lords of parliament two.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Prior to the 19th century peers also wore a matching crimson surcoat edged in miniver.

In 1953, "Peers taking part in the Processions or Ceremonies in Westminster Abbey" were directed to wear the Robe of State over [full-dress](/wiki/Full_Dress_uniform "Full Dress uniform") uniform ([Naval](/wiki/Uniforms_of_the_Royal_Navy "Uniforms of the Royal Navy"), [Military](/wiki/Uniforms_of_the_British_Army "Uniforms of the British Army"), [RAF](/wiki/Uniforms_of_the_Royal_Air_Force "Uniforms of the Royal Air Force") or [civil](/wiki/Court_uniform_and_dress_in_the_United_Kingdom#Court_uniform "Court uniform and dress in the United Kingdom")), if so entitled, or else over full [velvet court dress](/wiki/Court_uniform_and_dress_in_the_United_Kingdom#Men's_court_dress "Court uniform and dress in the United Kingdom") (or one of the alternative styles of Court Dress, as laid down in the Lord Chamberlain's regulations). Other peers in attendance were "expected to wear the same if possible"; but the wearing of [evening dress](/wiki/White_tie "White tie"), or a black suit with white bow tie, were also permitted (as was the use of a Parliament Robe or a mantle of one of the [Orders of Knighthood](/wiki/Orders_of_Knighthood "Orders of Knighthood") by those not taking part in the Processions or Ceremonies).[[133]](#cite_note-Gazette1-133)

In 2023, the dress code for peers was originally [lounge suits](/wiki/Lounge_suit "Lounge suit") or [parliamentary robes](/wiki/Parliamentary_robes "Parliamentary robes"),[[136]](#cite_note-136)[[137]](#cite_note-Diver-137) but this was changed in the week before the coronation after protests, with peers allowed to wear coronation robes but not coronets.[[138]](#cite_note-:6-138)

#### Peeresses' robes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Peeresses' robes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/George%2C_2nd_Earl_Harcourt%2C_his_wife_Elizabeth%2C_and_brother_William.jpg/250px-George%2C_2nd_Earl_Harcourt%2C_his_wife_Elizabeth%2C_and_brother_William.jpg)](/wiki/File:George,_2nd_Earl_Harcourt,_his_wife_Elizabeth,_and_brother_William.jpg)

An earl, wearing crimson robe and surcoat over court dress and holding his coronet, and countess, wearing crimson robe and kirtle over a court dress and wearing her coronet (1780). From a portrait of [George, 2nd Earl Harcourt](/wiki/George_Harcourt,_2nd_Earl_Harcourt "George Harcourt, 2nd Earl Harcourt") by Sir [Joshua Reynolds](/wiki/Joshua_Reynolds "Joshua Reynolds").

A peeress's coronation robe is described as a long ([trained](/wiki/Train_(clothing) "Train (clothing)")) crimson velvet [mantle](/wiki/Mantle_(clothing) "Mantle (clothing)"), edged all round with [miniver](/wiki/Miniver "Miniver") pure and having a cape of miniver pure (with rows of [ermine](/wiki/Ermine_(heraldry) "Ermine (heraldry)") indicating the rank of the wearer, as for peers).[[139]](#cite_note-Gazette2-139) Furthermore, the length of the train (and the width of the miniver edging) varies with the rank of the wearer: for duchesses, the trains are 1.8 m (2 yds) long, for marchionesses one and three-quarters yards, for countesses one and a half yards, for viscountesses one and a quarter yards, and for baronesses and ladies 90 cm (1 yd). The edgings are 13 cm (5 in) in width for duchesses, 10 cm (4 in) for marchionesses, 7.5 cm (3 in) for countesses and 5 cm (2 in) for viscountesses, baronesses and ladies.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

This Robe of State is directed to be worn with a sleeved crimson velvet [kirtle](/wiki/Kirtle "Kirtle"), which is similarly edged with miniver and worn over a full-length white or cream [court dress](/wiki/Court_uniform_and_dress_in_the_United_Kingdom#Women's_court_dress "Court uniform and dress in the United Kingdom") (without a train).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Headwear

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Headwear")]

During the Coronation, peers and peeresses formerly put on [coronets](/wiki/Coronets "Coronets"). Like their robes, their coronets are differentiated according to rank: the coronet of a duke or duchess is ornamented with eight strawberry leaves, that of a marquess or marchioness has four strawberry leaves alternating with four raised silver balls, that of an earl or countess eight strawberry leaves alternating with eight raised silver balls, that of a viscount or viscountess has sixteen smaller silver balls and that of a baron or baroness six silver balls. Peeresses' coronets are identical to those of peers, but smaller.[[140]](#cite_note-140) In addition, peeresses were told in 1953 that "a tiara should be worn, if possible".[[133]](#cite_note-Gazette1-133) The use of coronets was abolished for the 2023 coronation.

### Others

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Others")]

In 1953, those taking part in the [Procession inside the Abbey](/wiki/List_of_participants_in_the_coronation_procession_of_Elizabeth_II "List of participants in the coronation procession of Elizabeth II") who were not peers or peeresses were directed to wear [full-dress](/wiki/Full_Dress_uniform "Full Dress uniform") ([naval](/wiki/Uniforms_of_the_Royal_Navy "Uniforms of the Royal Navy"), [military](/wiki/Uniforms_of_the_British_Army "Uniforms of the British Army"), [air force](/wiki/Uniforms_of_the_Royal_Air_Force "Uniforms of the Royal Air Force") or [civil](/wiki/Court_uniform_and_dress_in_the_United_Kingdom#Court_uniform "Court uniform and dress in the United Kingdom")) uniform, or one of the forms of court dress laid down in the Lord Chamberlain's Regulations for Dress at Court. These regulations, as well as providing guidance for members of the public, specify forms of dress for a wide variety of office-holders and public officials, clergy, the judiciary, members of the Royal Household, etc. It also includes provision for [Scottish dress](/wiki/Scottish_dress "Scottish dress") to be worn.[[141]](#cite_note-141)

Officers in the Armed Forces and the Civil, Foreign, and Colonial Services who did not take part in the Procession wore uniform, and male civilians: "one of the forms of court dress as laid down in the Lord Chamberlain's Regulations for Dress at Court, or [evening dress](/wiki/White_tie "White tie") with knee breeches or trousers, or [morning dress](/wiki/Morning_dress "Morning dress"), or dark [lounge suits](/wiki/Lounge_suits "Lounge suits")".[[139]](#cite_note-Gazette2-139)

Ladies attending in 1953 were instructed to wear "evening dresses or afternoon dresses, with a light veiling falling from the back of the head". Coats and hats were not permitted but tiaras could be worn.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1953 an additional note made it clear that "Oriental dress may be worn by Ladies and Gentlemen for whom it is the usual Ceremonial Costume".[[139]](#cite_note-Gazette2-139)

## After-celebrations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: After-celebrations")]

Since the 20th century it has been traditional for the newly crowned monarch and other members of the royal family to sit for official portraits at [Buckingham Palace](/wiki/Buckingham_Palace "Buckingham Palace") and appear on the balcony, from where in 1953 they watched a flypast by the [Royal Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force").[[142]](#cite_note-142) During the appearance, the monarch wears the Imperial State Crown and, if there is one, the queen consort wears her [consort crown](/wiki/Consort_crown "Consort crown"). In the evening, a [fireworks](/wiki/Fireworks "Fireworks") display is held nearby, usually in [Hyde Park](/wiki/Hyde_Park,_London "Hyde Park, London").[[143]](#cite_note-143) In 1902, [Edward VII](/wiki/Edward_VII "Edward VII")'s illness led to the postponement of a fourteen-course banquet at Buckingham Palace.[[144]](#cite_note-Burnett2013-144) In 1953, two state banquets were held in the ballroom there, and classical music was provided by the [Royal Horse Guards](/wiki/Royal_Horse_Guards "Royal Horse Guards").[[145]](#cite_note-145)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/George_IV_coronation_banquet_1821.jpg/500px-George_IV_coronation_banquet_1821.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_IV_coronation_banquet_1821.jpg)

George IV's coronation banquet was held in [Westminster Hall](/wiki/Westminster_Hall "Westminster Hall") in the [Palace of Westminster](/wiki/Palace_of_Westminster "Palace of Westminster") in 1821; it was the last such banquet held.

Historically, the coronation was immediately followed by a banquet held in [Westminster Hall](/wiki/Westminster_Hall "Westminster Hall") in the [Palace of Westminster](/wiki/Palace_of_Westminster "Palace of Westminster") (which is also the home to the Houses of Parliament). The [King or Queen's Champion](/wiki/King%27s_Champion "King's Champion") (the office being held by the [Dymoke](/wiki/Dymoke "Dymoke") family in connection with the [Manor of Scrivelsby](/wiki/Manor_of_Scrivelsby "Manor of Scrivelsby")) would ride into the hall on horseback, wearing a knight's armour, with the Lord High Constable riding to his right and the Earl Marshal riding to his left. A herald would then make a proclamation of the readiness of the champion to fight anyone denying the monarch. After 1800, the form for this was as follows:[[146]](#cite_note-1911-coronation-146)

> If any person, of what degree soever, high or low, shall deny or gainsay our Sovereign Lord ..., King of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland, Defender of the Faith, son and next heir unto our Sovereign Lord the last King deceased, to be the right heir to the Imperial Crown of this Realm of Great Britain and Ireland, or that he ought not to enjoy the same; here is his Champion, who saith that he lieth, and is a false traitor, being ready in person to combat with him; and in this quarrel will adventure his life against him, on what day soever he shall be appointed.[[146]](#cite_note-1911-coronation-146)

The King's Champion would then throw down the gauntlet; the ceremony would be repeated at the centre of the hall and at the High Table (where the sovereign would be seated). The sovereign would then drink to the champion from a gold cup, which he would then present to the latter.[[146]](#cite_note-1911-coronation-146) This ritual was dropped from the [coronation of Queen Victoria](/wiki/Coronation_of_Queen_Victoria "Coronation of Queen Victoria") and was never revived. The offices of [Chief Butler of England](/wiki/Chief_Butler_of_England "Chief Butler of England"), [Grand Carver of England](/wiki/Grand_Carver_of_England "Grand Carver of England") and [Master Carver of Scotland](/wiki/The_Master_Carver "The Master Carver") were also associated with the coronation banquet.[[147]](#cite_note-keepers-147)

Banquets have not been held at Westminster Hall since the [coronation of George IV](/wiki/Coronation_of_George_IV "Coronation of George IV") in 1821. His coronation was the most elaborate in history; his brother and successor William IV eliminated the banquet on grounds of economy,[[26]](#cite_note-strong374-26) ending a 632-year-old tradition.[[148]](#cite_note-148) Since 1901, a Coronation [Fleet Review](/wiki/Fleet_review_(Commonwealth_realms) "Fleet review (Commonwealth realms)") has also been held. To celebrate the coronation, a [coronation honours list](/wiki/Coronation_honours "Coronation honours") is also released before the coronation.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Enthronement as Emperor of India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Enthronement as Emperor of India")]

Main article: [Delhi Durbar](/wiki/Delhi_Durbar "Delhi Durbar")

Queen Victoria assumed the title *[Empress of India](/wiki/Emperor_of_India "Emperor of India")* in 1876.[[149]](#cite_note-149) A [durbar](/wiki/Delhi_Durbar "Delhi Durbar") (court) was held in [Delhi](/wiki/Delhi "Delhi"), India on 1 January 1877 to proclaim her assumption of the title. The queen did not attend personally, but she was represented there by the [Viceroy](/wiki/Viceroy_of_India "Viceroy of India"), [Lord Lytton](/wiki/Robert_Bulwer-Lytton,_1st_Earl_of_Lytton "Robert Bulwer-Lytton, 1st Earl of Lytton").[[150]](#cite_note-150) A similar durbar was held on 1 January 1903 to celebrate the accession of Edward VII, who was represented by his brother the [Duke of Connaught](/wiki/Prince_Arthur,_Duke_of_Connaught_and_Strathearn "Prince Arthur, Duke of Connaught and Strathearn").[[151]](#cite_note-151) In 1911, [George V](/wiki/George_V "George V") also held a durbar which he and his wife [Queen Mary](/wiki/Mary_of_Teck "Mary of Teck") attended in person. Since it was deemed inappropriate for a Christian anointing and coronation to take place in a largely non-Christian nation, George V was not crowned in India; instead, he wore an [imperial crown](/wiki/Imperial_crown "Imperial crown") as he entered the Durbar. Tradition prohibited the removal of the Crown Jewels from the United Kingdom; therefore, a separate crown, known as the [Imperial Crown of India](/wiki/Imperial_Crown_of_India "Imperial Crown of India"), was created for him. The emperor was enthroned, and the Indian princes paid homage to him. Thereafter, certain political decisions, such as the decision to move the capital from [Calcutta](/wiki/Calcutta "Calcutta") to Delhi, were announced at the durbar. The ceremony was not repeated, and the imperial title was abandoned by George VI in 1948, 10 months after [India gained independence](/wiki/Indian_Independence_Act_1947 "Indian Independence Act 1947").[[152]](#cite_note-152)

## Kings of Arms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coronation_of_the_British_monarch&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Kings of Arms")]

Aside from kings and queens, the only individuals authorised to wear crowns (as opposed to coronets) are the [Kings of Arms](/wiki/Kings_of_Arms "Kings of Arms"), the United Kingdom's senior [heraldic officials](/wiki/Officer_of_arms "Officer of arms").[[153]](#cite_note-chambers-153) Like the peers' coronets, these crowns are only put on at the actual moment of the monarch's crowning, after which they are worn for the rest of the service and its subsequent festivities. [Garter](/wiki/Garter_Principal_King_of_Arms "Garter Principal King of Arms"), [Clarenceaux](/wiki/Clarenceaux_King_of_Arms "Clarenceaux King of Arms"), and [Norroy and Ulster](/wiki/Norroy_and_Ulster_King_of_Arms "Norroy and Ulster King of Arms") Kings of Arms have heraldic jurisdiction over England, Wales and Northern Ireland;[[154]](#cite_note-154) Lord Lyon King of Arms is responsible for Scotland.[[155]](#cite_note-155) In addition, there is a King of Arms attached to each of the [Order of the Bath](/wiki/Order_of_the_Bath "Order of the Bath"), [Order of St. Michael and St. George](/wiki/Order_of_St._Michael_and_St._George "Order of St. Michael and St. George") and the [Order of the British Empire](/wiki/Order_of_the_British_Empire "Order of the British Empire"). These have only a ceremonial role, but are authorised by the statutes of their orders to wear the same crown as Garter at a coronation.[[156]](#cite_note-156) The crown of a King of Arms is silver-gilt and consists of sixteen [acanthus leaves](/wiki/Acanthus_leaves "Acanthus leaves") alternating in height, and inscribed with the words *Miserere mei Deus secundum magnam misericordiam tuam* (Latin: "Have mercy on me O God according to Thy great mercy", from Psalm 51).[[153]](#cite_note-chambers-153) The [Lord Lyon King of Arms](/wiki/Lord_Lyon_King_of_Arms "Lord Lyon King of Arms") has worn a crown of this style at all coronations since that of [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III"). Prior to that he wore a replica of the Crown of Scotland. In 2004 a new replica of this crown was created for use by the Lord Lyon.[[157]](#cite_note-157)
